title: Interest premium and external position: A state dependent approach
journal: Journal of International Financial Markets, Institutions & Money
summary_en: Countries with large external-debt positions have historically paid an interest premium on sovereign borrowing — but how strong is this effect, and does it depend on the macroeconomic state? The authors estimate the relationship in a state-dependent framework that lets the interest-premium–external-debt elasticity differ by regime (normal vs stressed). The premium is much steeper in stressed periods than in normal ones: a given increase in external liabilities costs much more when the economy is already fragile. A state-linear model understates crisis-time debt costs.
data_used: Panel of sovereign interest spreads, external-debt positions, and macro controls for a set of advanced and emerging economies over roughly 1990–2018. State definition via macro stress indicators.
policy_relevance: Direct input for Hungarian fiscal-risk assessment (ÁKK, Pénzügyminisztérium) and for MNB FX-reserve adequacy. Supports state-contingent debt-sustainability frameworks: a debt-to-GDP ratio that is safe in a normal regime can be costly in a stressed one. Caveat: state-dependence is econometrically identified in-sample; out-of-sample stress regimes may differ.
